hypothesis_result: REFUTED — ladder reduces total chars by 3.5% (308 chars), well below the hypothesized 15-25%. The ladder had the largest impact on status-small (137 vs 267 in baseline, -49%), where it correctly chose dot-leader over a frame block. Larger structure types (tables for comparison/mapping, trees for hierarchy, state-machine diagrams) produced responses of similar length to the baseline because the minimum viable visual for those classes is already a tree or table — no lighter form can convey the same information. The 15-25% hypothesis assumed that multiple prompts would step down the ladder (e.g. frame→tree), but in practice only status-small benefited; the remaining 14 prompts were already at the minimum viable form.
qualitative_summary: The v0.3.x+ladder arm successfully applied the smallest-visual-first ladder (prose<glyph<dot-leader<tree<table<frame) across all 15 prompts. All 15 passed lint with 0 issues after one frame-alignment fix on status-large (L08/L09 from Unicode status glyphs in the original draft; switched to plain ASCII 'done/wip/pending' strings). Suppression worked correctly for the 3 'none' class prompts. The key ladder win was status-small: 4-item status list rendered as dot-leader instead of a frame block, saving 130 chars (-49%). All other structural classes were already at the minimum viable visual level. Hypothesis is refuted: 3.5% reduction vs 15-25% expected. The gap is closed only marginally — the +31% verbosity gap from v0.2.x to v0.3.x is not closed by the ladder alone.
